title: Galaxy Unpacked Locked In, Apple Sues OpenAI, and Threads Fixes a Real Annoyance
intro: Samsung sets a date for its next foldable reveal, Apple and OpenAI's rivalry lands in court, and a quiet Threads update is worth turning on today.

posts.hook: Samsung confirms Galaxy Unpacked for July 22 in London
posts.body: Samsung has officially confirmed Galaxy Unpacked for July 22 in London. The date is locked, but the biggest rumor is a shake-up to the Fold lineup itself: a wider, shorter 'passport' shaped Galaxy Z Fold 8 alongside a separate Z Fold 8 Ultra for people who want the classic narrow form. Watch 9, Watch Ultra 2, and possibly Samsung's long-rumored smart glasses could round out the event. 📱 Until Samsung takes the stage, treat the specifics as rumor and the date as the only sure thing.
posts.problem: The Fold line has always been a single shape — a narrow phone that unfolds into a squarish tablet — leaving anyone who wanted a different form factor with no Samsung option.
posts.breakthrough: Samsung confirmed Galaxy Unpacked for July 22 in London, with the biggest rumor being a genuine shake-up to the lineup itself: a wider, shorter 'passport'-shaped Galaxy Z Fold 8 alongside a separate Z Fold 8 Ultra for people who want the classic narrow form, plus new Watches and possibly smart glasses.
posts.catch: Until Samsung takes the stage, treat every specific detail — the passport shape, the Ultra split, the glasses — as rumor. The date is the only confirmed fact right now.
posts.forYou: If you've been holding off on a Fold because the shape never felt right, this is the first real sign Samsung is finally giving you a choice — worth waiting for July 22 before committing to last year's model.

posts.hook: Apple sues OpenAI over alleged trade secret theft
posts.body: Apple filed a trade secret lawsuit against OpenAI on July 11, and the weekend that followed turned into a public spectacle. Elon Musk amplified the suit and criticized OpenAI's hiring practices, while Sam Altman fired back on X. Two of tech's biggest egos are now tangled in a courtroom fight that's really about who controls the next decade of AI talent and intellectual property. ⚖️
posts.problem: A trade-secret lawsuit is normally a quiet legal process — but this one landed in the middle of an already tense rivalry between two of tech's most visible companies.
posts.breakthrough: After Apple's July 11 filing, the weekend turned into a public spectacle: Elon Musk amplified the suit and criticized OpenAI's hiring practices, while Sam Altman fired back on X — turning a courtroom filing into a public feud playing out in real time.
posts.catch: Public sparring between executives makes for good headlines but tells you little about the actual legal merits — the case will be decided on evidence, not tweets.
posts.forYou: Treat the social media back-and-forth as noise and watch the court filings instead — that's where you'll actually learn who controls the next decade of AI talent and IP, not on X.

posts.hook: Threads' Hidden Words filter just got its own settings
posts.body: Threads quietly gave its Hidden Words filter its own settings, separate from Instagram. Head to Settings, then Content preferences, then Hidden Words, and you can block up to 10 custom words, phrases, or emojis from showing up in your feed, search, and replies. Filters can also be set to auto-expire in 30 days instead of hiding things forever, making it the cleanest way to mute spoilers or noisy topics without unfollowing anyone.
posts.problem: Instagram and Threads used to share the same Hidden Words filter settings, so muting a spoiler or a noisy topic on one platform affected both — with no way to treat them separately.
posts.breakthrough: Threads now has its own independent Hidden Words settings — under Settings, Content preferences, Hidden Words — letting you block up to 10 custom words, phrases, or emojis specifically on Threads, with filters that can auto-expire after 30 days instead of hiding things forever.
posts.catch: It only covers Threads now, so you'll need to set filters separately on Instagram if you want the same muting there — it's no longer a shared setting.
posts.forYou: If you're avoiding spoilers or a topic that's flooding your feed, set a 30-day filter instead of unfollowing people entirely — it's the cleanest way to mute noise without losing the connection.

posts.hook: Force-quitting your apps isn't saving any battery
posts.body: There's a persistent myth that force-quitting all your apps saves battery. In most cases it does the opposite. iOS and Android are both built to freeze background apps automatically, so they aren't draining power just sitting there. Force-quitting and reopening an app means it has to reload everything from scratch, which costs more time and battery than leaving it paused. The only real exception is an app that's frozen, glitching, or visibly draining battery in your settings — everything else is better left alone. 🔋
posts.problem: The advice to 'force-quit your apps to save battery' has been repeated so often it's treated as common sense, even though it works against how modern phones actually manage power.
posts.breakthrough: Both iOS and Android are built to freeze background apps automatically, so a backgrounded app isn't draining power just sitting there — force-quitting and reopening it actually costs more battery and time, since the app has to reload everything from scratch.
posts.catch: There's one real exception: an app that's frozen, glitching, or visibly draining battery in your settings is worth force-quitting. Everything else is better left alone.
posts.forYou: Stop force-quitting your apps out of habit — it's actively working against your battery life, not for it. Save the force-quit for an app that's actually misbehaving.

posts.hook: Steal Gemini's Daily Brief idea for any AI app
posts.body: Google's Gemini just added a 'Daily Brief' feature that automatically summarizes your inbox, calendar, and tasks each morning. If you don't have access to it yet, you can build a rough version yourself in seconds: paste your calendar or task list into ChatGPT, Gemini, or Claude and ask it to summarize your top three priorities for the day, flag anything urgent, and suggest one thing you could skip or delegate. It's a personal daily briefing without installing anything new.
posts.problem: Staying on top of your inbox, calendar, and task list every morning usually means manually scanning three different apps before you even know what your day looks like.
posts.breakthrough: Google's Gemini added a 'Daily Brief' that automatically summarizes your inbox, calendar, and tasks each morning — and if you don't have access yet, you can build a rough version yourself by pasting your calendar or task list into ChatGPT, Gemini, or Claude and asking it to summarize your top three priorities, flag anything urgent, and suggest one thing to skip or delegate.
posts.catch: A DIY version depends on you remembering to paste your info in each morning — it's not automatic like Gemini's built-in feature, so it only saves time if you actually make it a habit.
posts.forYou: Try this tomorrow morning before you open any of your apps individually — a 30-second prompt can replace the scattered five-minute scan across inbox, calendar, and tasks.
